generations.code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    if n == 1:
        print("1")
        return
    
    result = ['0'] * n
    players = list(range(n))
    
    for start in range(n):
        max_a = a[start]
        max_b = b[start]
        count = 1
        changed = True
        while changed:
            changed = False
            for i in range(n):
                if i == start:
                    continue
                if a[i] < max_a or b[i] < max_b:
                    if a[i] > max_a or b[i] > max_b:
                        changed = True
                    new_max_a = max(max_a, a[i])
                    new_max_b = max(max_b, b[i])
                    if new_max_a != max_a or new_max_b != max_b:
                        changed = True
                    max_a = new_max_a
                    max_b = new_max_b
                    count += 1  # wrong counting
        # need proper BFS
    
    print(''.join(result))

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()

bugs.description: The result array is never updated; the algorithm never marks any player as a possible winner, so output is all zeros (except n==1).
The condition `if a[i] < max_a or b[i] < max_b:` is the opposite of the intended logic; it should check for players that are stronger on at least one map.
Inside the previous `if`, the nested condition `if a[i] > max_a or b[i] > max_b:` can never be true because the outer condition guarantees the opposite; this makes the `changed` flag logic incorrect.
The `count` variable is incremented for each examined player but never used; it is also incorrectly counted (should count visited players).
The while‑loop may become infinite for certain inputs because `changed` can be set to True repeatedly without making progress, due to the flawed condition logic.
Overall algorithm complexity is O(n³) (outer loop over start, inner loop over all players inside a while loop), which will TLE for n up to 1e5.
`players` list is created but never used, indicating incomplete implementation.
The result array is never updated; the algorithm never marks any player as a possible winner, so output is all zeros (except n==1).
The condition `if a[i] < max_a or b[i] < max_b:` is the opposite of the intended logic; it should check for players that are stronger on at least one map.
Inside the previous `if`, the nested condition `if a[i] > max_a or b[i] > max_b:` can never be true because the outer condition guarantees the opposite; this makes the `changed` flag logic incorrect.
The `count` variable is incremented for each examined player but never used; it is also incorrectly counted (should count visited players).
The while‑loop may become infinite for certain inputs because `changed` can be set to True repeatedly without making progress, due to the flawed condition logic.
Overall algorithm complexity is O(n³) (outer loop over start, inner loop over all players inside a while loop), which will TLE for n up to 1e5.
`players` list is created but never used, indicating incomplete implementation.

fixes.code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    if n == 1:
        print("1")
        return
    
    result = ['0'] * n
    
    # Sort players by a-value
    order = sorted(range(n), key=lambda i: a[i])
    # order[0] has smallest a, order[n-1] has largest a
    
    # For player p to win: simulate greedy expansion
    # Process players in order of a. Start with p's stats.
    # For each player in increasing a order (excluding p):
    #   if a[i] < max_a: can beat on map1, absorb -> update max_b
    #   else: need b[i] < max_b to beat on map2, absorb -> update max_a
    
    for idx in range(n):
        p = order[idx]
        max_a = a[p]
        max_b = b[p]
        can_win = True
        # Process all players with a < a[p] first (they all have a < max_a, so beatable)
        # Then players with a > a[p]
        # Players with a < a[p]: always beatable, update max_b
        for j in range(idx):  # these have a < a[p]
            i = order[j]
            max_b = max(max_b, b[i])
        # Players with a > a[p]: need b[i] < max_b at time of encounter
        for j in range(idx + 1, n):
            i = order[j]
            # a[i] > max_a currently, need b[i] < max_b
            if b[i] < max_b:
                max_a = a[i]
                max_b = max(max_b, b[i])
            else:
                can_win = False
                break
        if can_win:
            result[p] = '1'
    
    print(''.join(result))

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()

pipelines.final_code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    if n == 1:
        print("1")
        return
    
    result = ['0'] * n
    
    # Sort players by a-value
    order = sorted(range(n), key=lambda i: a[i])
    # order[0] has smallest a, order[n-1] has largest a
    
    # For player p to win: simulate greedy expansion
    # Process players in order of a. Start with p's stats.
    # For each player in increasing a order (excluding p):
    #   if a[i] < max_a: can beat on map1, absorb -> update max_b
    #   else: need b[i] < max_b to beat on map2, absorb -> update max_a
    
    for idx in range(n):
        p = order[idx]
        max_a = a[p]
        max_b = b[p]
        can_win = True
        # Process all players with a < a[p] first (they all have a < max_a, so beatable)
        # Then players with a > a[p]
        # Players with a < a[p]: always beatable, update max_b
        for j in range(idx):  # these have a < a[p]
            i = order[j]
            max_b = max(max_b, b[i])
        # Players with a > a[p]: need b[i] < max_b at time of encounter
        for j in range(idx + 1, n):
            i = order[j]
            # a[i] > max_a currently, need b[i] < max_b
            if b[i] < max_b:
                max_a = a[i]
                max_b = max(max_b, b[i])
            else:
                can_win = False
                break
        if can_win:
            result[p] = '1'
    
    print(''.join(result))

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()
